title: A* Search: Open Set, Closed Set, and f-score
subtitle: A* finds paths by balancing the cost already paid with a heuristic estimate of what remains.
summary: The path finder project visualizes A* through grid cells, obstacles, heuristics, and the final path.

artifact.title: Frontier expansion on a grid
artifact.description: Switch heuristics to see how the explored cells and final path differ.

contentBlocks.text: A* behaves like a careful explorer with a map estimate. It keeps a frontier of possible next cells, scores each candidate, and always expands the candidate that looks cheapest after combining what it already paid with what it still expects to pay.

contentBlocks.content:
$$f(n)=g(n)+h(n)$$

- $g(n)$ is the real cost from the start to node $n$.
- $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from node $n$ to the goal.
- $f(n)$ is the priority score used to choose what to expand next.

1. DiscoverPut neighboring cells into the open set with tentative scores.
2. PrioritizePick the open cell with the lowest f-score.
3. CommitMove that cell to the closed set after considering its neighbors.
4. ReconstructWhen the goal is reached, follow parent pointers backward.

contentBlocks.title: Why the heuristic condition matters
contentBlocks.body: If the heuristic never overestimates the true remaining cost, A* keeps its shortest-path guarantee. If it overestimates, it may become faster but no longer reliably optimal.

rows: Fits four-direction grid movement and produces rectilinear search pressure
Fits continuous geometry or diagonal movement better
Can be fast but may skip the optimal path

sections.heading: The Score That Drives the Search
body: A* gives each candidate node an f-score: f(n) = g(n) + h(n).
g(n) is the known cost from the start to the node. h(n) is the estimated cost from the node to the goal. The algorithm repeatedly expands the node with the smallest f-score.
sections.callout: A* is powerful because h(n) gives the search a sense of direction without discarding the actual path cost.

sections.heading: Open and Closed Sets
body: The open set stores frontier nodes that might still lead to the best path. The closed set stores nodes already expanded.
Walls or obstacles are never expanded. The final path appears when the goal is reached and parent pointers are traced backward.

rows: Candidates discovered but not fully expanded
Nodes whose neighbors have already been considered
Parent chain from goal back to start
Blocked cell that cannot be traversed

pitfalls: Using a heuristic that overestimates and breaks shortest-path guarantees.
Forgetting to update a node when a cheaper route is found.
Confusing visited cells with the final path.
Using Euclidean distance on a grid where only four-direction movement is allowed without thinking through the movement model.

quiz.question: What does f(n) combine?
options: Known cost plus estimated remaining cost
